findings.description: ReDoS: name_pattern is an attacker-supplied regex compiled and run (re.search) against every result row with no timeout, so a catastrophic-backtracking pattern (e.g. '(a+)+$') pins a CPU core and denial-of-services the endpoint (CWE-1333/CWE-400); the automated scanner missed this.

Scanner-flagged Log Injection (CWE-117): the only attacker-influenced value is `page`, formatted via `%d` with logging's lazy args, so CRLF/newline forging is not actually reachable as written — risk is largely mitigated; latent only if the format spec is ever changed to %s or the unenforced `page: int` annotation is violated.

Missing input-domain validation (CWE-20 / A06): `page` and `page_size` have no lower bound, so a negative `page` yields a negative `offset` and Python slicing fails open by silently returning a different window from the end of the list instead of erroring, and `min(page_size, MAX)` does not reject negative page_size.

Unhandled exceptional condition (CWE-754 / A10): matcher.search(u['display_name']) assumes display_name is non-null; a single row with NULL display_name raises TypeError and breaks every pattern search (availability/DoS via data condition).

No authorization/ownership check at the data-access boundary (A01/CWE-639): deactivate() soft-deletes any user by ID and by_email() discloses any user's record; whether this is exploitable depends on caller-side authz which is out of scope (no other files in scope), reported as a design observation.
